title: Arbiter of the Benchmark
description: The Arbiter runs a live diagnostic on the party's performance history. All six worlds of data. The numbers are anomalous.

steps.text: The chamber beyond the Firewall was not a room. It was a process space: no walls, just bounded execution. Data columns floated in every direction. At the center, the Arbiter of the Benchmark sat cross-legged in the air, surrounded by cascading readouts that were all, visibly, about the party. Their entire history, displayed in real time.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Benchmark
lines.text: Running diagnostic. Your performance will be measured against optimal baseline.

lines.speaker: Cleric
lines.text: We saw something like this in World 3. The extrapolated baseline.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Benchmark
lines.text: The World 3 baseline was extrapolated. This baseline is not. I have your complete combat logs from every world. Fifty-three encounters. I have built the optimal from your own data.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Benchmark
lines.text: You have played suboptimally in thirty-one of those encounters. You have also survived all fifty-three. This is the anomaly I cannot explain.

lines.speaker: Mage
lines.text: Maybe suboptimal and survivable aren't mutually exclusive.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Benchmark
lines.text: The model disagrees. I have run it four thousand times. Statistically, you should have failed at twenty-seven distinct points. You did not fail. The model is either wrong or you are wrong. I intend to determine which.

lines.speaker: Rogue
lines.text: And if the model is wrong?

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Benchmark
lines.text: Then I update the model. This is acceptable. I prefer accurate models to comfortable ones. Diagnostic: commencing.
